title: 22-year-old man with right iliac fossa pain
candidateInstructions: You are an FY1 doctor in the ED surgical assessment unit. Mr Jake Morrison, a 22-year-old student, presents with a 6-hour history of abdominal pain. Please take a focused history, request appropriate investigations, and formulate a differential diagnosis.
examinerNotes: Classic acute appendicitis: central pain migrating to RIF, anorexia, nausea, low-grade fever. Alvarado score >7. Candidate should not give opioids withholding — current guidance says analgesia does not mask signs. Needs surgical review.

patient.name: Jake Morrison
patient.presentingComplaint: Stomach pain that started in the middle and moved to the right side
patient.reasonForAttendance: I've had bad stomach pain all day and it keeps getting worse

hpi.site: Started around the belly button, now mainly bottom right of stomach
hpi.exacerbatingFactors: Moving, walking, pressing on the area

systemsReview.gastrointestinal: No diarrhoea, last opened bowels yesterday

examination.abdominal: Maximal tenderness McBurney's point. Rovsing's sign positive. Rebound tenderness RIF. Guarding. Psoas sign positive.

investigations.name: Full Blood Count
investigations.interpretation: Leukocytosis with neutrophilia — consistent with acute appendicitis.

investigations.name: C-Reactive Protein
investigations.interpretation: Elevated CRP supporting acute inflammatory process.

investigations.name: Urinalysis (urine dipstick)
investigations.result: Trace leucocytes. No nitrites. No blood. No glucose. No protein. May see sterile pyuria with adjacent appendicitis.
investigations.interpretation: Near-normal urinalysis. Trace leucocytes may represent proximity of appendix to ureter — does not confirm UTI.

investigations.name: Abdominal Ultrasound
investigations.result: Non-compressible appendix 9mm diameter with periappendiceal fat stranding. Findings consistent with acute appendicitis. No perforation seen. No free fluid.
investigations.interpretation: ACUTE APPENDICITIS confirmed on ultrasound. Dilated non-compressible appendix.

patient.emotionalState: In pain, anxious about what is wrong. Hoping it is nothing serious. Worried about missing university exams.
patient.communicationStyle: Direct, answers questions clearly. Young man, slightly embarrassed about the pain.
hiddenConcerns: Could this be my appendix? My friend had to have his out
I have exams in 2 weeks — will I be okay by then?
doNotVolunteer: Anorexia — only mention if asked specifically about appetite
That the pain started in the middle and moved — only if asked where it started
No testicular pain — only if directly asked about testicular symptoms

dataGathering.criterion: Establishes pain history with SOCRATES including migration
dataGathering.detailedExpectation: Elicits central-to-RIF migration — pathognomonic feature of appendicitis

dataGathering.criterion: Asks about anorexia and gastrointestinal symptoms
dataGathering.detailedExpectation: Specifically asks about appetite, nausea, vomiting, bowel habit change

dataGathering.criterion: Asks about urinary symptoms to rule out renal colic

dataGathering.detailedExpectation: Rules out testicular torsion in young man

clinicalManagement.detailedExpectation: States appendicitis as most likely diagnosis
References RIF tenderness, elevated temperature, leukocytosis, migration, anorexia
IV access, fluids, analgesia, NBM, surgical review, IV antibiotics pre-op

interpersonalSkills.criterion: Empathy for young patient in pain

interpersonalSkills.detailedExpectation: Explains likely appendicitis, investigations, possible surgery

learningObjectives: Recognise classic migration of pain as hallmark of appendicitis
Apply Alvarado score for clinical probability
Rule out differential diagnoses with targeted history
Plan surgical management and appropriate investigations
